column2: Greg was too young for that role.
Fumitaka sat bewildered, trying to figure out who was where, while Marta sat beside him — unfamiliar, very pregnant and, on top of that, utterly phlegmatic.
For the last six months she'd desperately wanted to sleep, and now she no longer needed to shout 'I don't care' — it was obvious as it was.
When Miyechka went into the kitchen to put on the tea, Fumitaka darted after her.
He crept up so quietly that Miyechka got frightened when she turned around, and out of fright bowed to him diligently.
Fumitaka bowed too, always ready to follow the rules of the house where he was a guest.
He explained to Miyechka that he just couldn't pick the moment to ask for Marta's hand, and couldn't identify her parents.
— Oh, nonsense, — said Miyechka.
— Ask the grandmother.
Fumitaka came out onto the terrace with the teapot and said: 'Madam.'
— Madam, — Miyechka translated.
Fumitaka was addressing Nati as he said this, because he thought Nati was Marta's grandmother: both had dark eyes, and besides, he'd noticed that the roots of Nati's hair were dark too, like Marta's.
Marko was pounding the dinosaur with a hammer.
Nati was shouting something into the grandmother's ear.
Fumitaka thought she was translating.
— I would like, on this sunny day...
— Fumitaka said, nervously.
— I would like, — Miyechka translated briefly.
— Marko!
— Maria exclaimed.
— Leave that dinosaur alone for at least a minute!
Marko pounded with the hammer in concentration.
The plaster crumbled and flew into the salad.
Lilichka automatically held her palm by the salad, looking at Fumitaka in order to give him the floor after all.
Roberto was bawling, because the front leg had broken off his dinosaur.
And he was bawling because it was Marko who'd knocked that leg off.
Marko's dinosaur was all battered.
— What a stupid toy.
— said the grandmother.
— Mom, it's archaeological, — Maria whispered, while Fumitaka spoke.
— We met…
— By the coffee machine, — Miyechka went on for him with a smile.
Marta, somewhere deep in her soul, was happy and even wanted to get married already.
She looked at Fumitaka and his hapless attempts to ask for her hand — for some reason from Lilichka's mother-in-law — with love and a tenderness strange to herself.
But on the surface of her soul she desperately wanted to sleep.

The dinosaurs were being pounded, Nati shouted, Lilichka shielded the salad with her palm, and Fumitaka asked Nati for Marta's hand, and she, though seeing Marta for the first time, joyfully gave her consent.
Although Marta gave no such consent.
She looked at Fumitaka indifferently and didn't even raise an eyebrow, the way only she and the grandmother could.
Nati
At the table Marta didn't say a word, even though Nati had given Fumitaka permission to marry her.
She thought only about when she could get up from the table and go to sleep.

Marta had never believed in the romance of such moments, so she wasn't even vexed that her pale, blue-veined hand had been requested from a woman who was a stranger to her, and that children were shrieking in the background.
The children scuffled on the grass, tearing at each other's hair.
Nati looked at them with tenderness and said something about happiness.
Grandmother Teya restrained herself from starting to pull the children apart, because Nati was admiring them and she didn't want to spoil her pleasure.
The two women sat side by side on a bench they'd dragged closer to the terrace railings, and talked about something.
And although they had no common language, their experience, their fates, their weariness and at the same time their love for family were their common language.
Although it was mostly Nati who talked.
She had always dreamed of a big family — almost hundred-year-old parents, with cousins, sisters and brothers sitting at the table.
With headstrong, homely teenagers, doll-like five-year-olds and children not yet born.

There had been such a thing only in her childhood, and then their whole family was scattered across continents, the children forgot Spanish, the parents, clutching their hearts, died in the houses of various children before they were even sixty.
Nati herself had married an Irishman, who turned out to be utterly unfit for life with a woman whose hair was scorched by desert winds, and later fled somewhere to Norway and never came back.
— I hope he's dead, — Nati would say.
Greg, as luck would have it, looked terribly like his father, and Roberto looked like Greg.
'Some kind of curse,' Nati would sometimes hiss.
And only in Marko, who was not her blood grandson, did Nati see the image of those boys of her childhood who ran about the dusty yard.
Now she sat amid a big family.
She liked the dark-haired Marta very much.
She'd gotten used to Lilichka and respected her for managing, despite her apparent weakness, to give birth to the sturdy Marko, even if not to her son.
The fair Miyechka she loved for her Spanish name and for not using sunscreen and turning chocolate-brown in the sun, despite her white hair.
And Maria she loved for loving Greg, despite the fact that he hadn't yet managed to make Lilichka another sturdy boy, or at least a girl, but had instead made the delicate Roberto.
On seeing Lusya — thin and fair — Nati immediately reconciled herself to the fact that someone else would take her, but still expressed to Greg her hope that Lusya would end up with them.
Nati sat beside Teodora, happy, watching the sinewy, sturdy Marko defeat the delicate angel Roberto, until grandmother Teya got up and asked Marko to take pity on his stepbrother.
He got up from the grass, flashed his prickly black eyes at her and, shouting: 'Déjame en paz!' — ran off.
Nati looked at him in such a way that it seemed little hearts were about to spill from her eyes, like in cartoons.

Lilichka, having caught up with Marko by a bush, dropped to her knees in front of him so their faces were at the same level, and murmured something to him inspiredly and at length, until the boy went up to grandmother Teya and said: 'Sorry!'
As he did so, he butted angrily into her hand.
The grandmother stroked his coarse hair and kissed his forehead.
Roberto, all this time, lay on his back and watched the clouds gradually turn pink in the sky and evening descend on the earth.
Marta fell asleep almost immediately, having repeated to Fumitaka that he should take the children to hell and clear off himself.
Fumitaka sat beside her and watched her breasts rise — they'd suddenly grown larger, become firm and not at all like the ones he'd known.
He looked at her face, which had also become different.
He was a little shorter than Marta, and now it looked as if she'd become twice his size, but she seemed to him a beautiful, unearthly, if large, creature.
He didn't dare touch her hand and only watched as her belly, stretched tight under the white T-shirt, twitched now and then.
Then she would sigh and sink back into sleep.
Gradually darkness descended, and Fumitaka, having put his own sweater under his head, lay down on the floor, not daring to disturb Marta.
Through her sleep she heard his breathing, and she dreamed that she had no belly and was swimming, swimming, swimming.
And beside her swam a large fish that breathed exactly like Fumitaka — almost silently.
Summer had passed its midpoint, and the second half of summer, as everyone knows, is three times shorter.
After it, summer no longer goes on, but races swiftly to its end.
Lilya and Solomiya sat side by side.
Maria had taken all the children with her and led them to the water to watch the river swallow the sun.
The children followed her in wonder, their bare feet getting tangled in the grass, they hurried so as not to miss the river's supper — a big, round, red sun like a Christmas-tree ornament.
— Solomiya, it's good to breathe freely, isn't it? — asked Lilichka.
And when she called her by her full name, it invariably meant the conversation would be serious.
— I suppose, — she answered.
— But this freedom is so heavy.
— What do you mean?
— asked Miyechka.

— It's so hard to resolve on anything, so hard to realize that there are hundreds of paths before you, — Lilichka had drunk three glasses of wine at supper, and the world suddenly seemed all too clear to her.
— Don't exaggerate, Lilya, — Mia answered, not because she disagreed with her sister, but because she simply sometimes lacked the courage to dramatize everything so openly.
— You have only two paths: Canberra and not-Canberra.
— Before, I couldn't have decided.
Just as you couldn't have decided.
— And what am I deciding?
— asked Miyechka.
— And you're choosing between eternal suffering, which is achingly beautiful to you, and eternal peace, which bores you to the point of dizziness.
— You're dramatizing, — Miyechka replied.
— And you know, — Lilya went on, — you know there's this method: toss a coin, and while it's in the air, you'll know for sure what you want more.
— We've already drawn lots for a child with matches, — Solomiya replied.
— And look, we in this house are all losers of a kind, — Lilichka's dramatic mood was simply impossible to interrupt.

— I'm stuck between continents and children, of whom I suddenly have three, you're stuck between two people, and without resolving that you can't move on, Marta tried to run away and is now on the threshold of change, Mom is discovering a new free life for herself, but at the same time she's also on the threshold of becoming the one in charge here.
She knows it's her responsibility, and the grandmother is on a threshold too…
— Don't go on, — Miyechka covered her ears.
She knew that Lilichka was telling the truth about everyone.
At times she was merciless.
— It's a shelter, Solomiya.
It's a refuge for cowards whom the current carries off somewhere, and they don't know where they need to go, and they just want to climb out onto the bank for a moment and dry off.
Here we are, you and I, sitting, shaking ourselves off.
Miyechka sat hugging her knees.
And then at last she said:
— Do you remember when grandfather was already ill?
— Mm-hm.
— And he sent us to a field he'd never had.
— Yes.
— To his imaginary field, which maybe his great-grandfather had had.
And you and I would go to that 'field,' hide behind the house and wait there.
— Yes, Mia, this shelter is imaginary.
There is no field.
Nothing stops, no matter how much we pretend we're putting life on pause.
The next morning, while the still-gentle sun lit up that part of the garden by the house where a human foot could still tread, Lilichka suggested that Marta and Fumitaka have their photo taken.
Fumitaka, as always, folded in half in a gesture of gratitude, while Marta rolled her eyes as usual.
Lilichka convinced Marta that the children, whoever they'd be with, would have at least one family photo.

Lilichka, in defiance of the fashion that dictated photographing everyone in motion, loved photographs like those in an old family album, where faces were turned three-quarters and women stood beside the men, a hand on their shoulder, while the men sat in armchairs.
She seated Fumitaka in a garden chair.
He'd put on a snow-white shirt and looked as if he'd stepped out of a brochure about a healthy lifestyle.
Marta stood beside the chair, tired, with half-closed eyes, and her pale hand lay on her future husband's white shirt.
Fumitaka wanted to get married before the children were born.
And no one supported him in this, and most importantly — Marta herself.
No one in the family dared express an opinion on this, afraid of provoking Marta's fury — everyone just wanted Marta to carry the children to term.

Fumitaka's only ally was Mrs. Darochka, who, however hard she strained to remember his name, never managed to, but insisted to grandmother Teya in a mysterious whisper that could be heard throughout the house that if he didn't marry her now, then with two children he certainly wouldn't, and that in such a condition you don't turn up your nose, but go where you need to, even if you have to give birth in a field.
The grandmother nodded and was only afraid that Lilichka would hear the conversation, because she would immediately come in and say everything she thought.
But Lilichka was just then taking photos, and when she took photos, no one and nothing existed for her.
Miyechka lay on the grass, looking at the photo session and the doomed Marta, while hugging Roberto.
This gentle creature with straw-colored hair was somehow dear to her, and they spoke in a strange mixture of English, Ukrainian and French.
Miyechka knew that these languages were related, and so in French there were bound to be Spanish words.
It was a search for common words, and they both enjoyed it.
Roberto lay there, looked at the sky and said:
— Salchicha!
— Sausage!
— Miyechka laughed.
— Yes!
— Roberto rejoiced, pronouncing the letter 't' with an aspiration.
Nati went with Greg to the market and looked there for at least some decoration for the terrace, because right now it was 'like an orphanage.'
In the garden section the market was overrun with gnomes, mushrooms and spotted fawns.
None of this stuff suited Nati at all.
— Señora!
— she exclaimed, coming up to the next stall.
— Señora!
Greg didn't go with his mother, trying to avoid misunderstandings and embarrassment.
He was exactly as shy as his father, who was now somewhere in the Norwegian mountains herding sheep, unable to overcome the trauma of living with Nati.
— Here's one, — said a mustachioed seller in an unbuttoned shirt with a cigarette in the corner of his mouth.
He carried out from behind the counter some Snow White figure, holding it by the head.
— Que no, no, — Nati grimaced.
— No!
Señora!
— and she pointed to her own breasts and the place that had once been a waist.
Nati was dressed in a wide black blouse, white trousers and dark glasses.
Then the seller beckoned her with a gesture, and Nati followed him.
At the end of the next row, behind the pruning shears and the pseudo-Greek flowerpots, there they were — the naked women.
They lay, sat and bent over.
Nati chose the woman with the biggest breasts.
The busty woman, made in China, bent over and therefore with a fold on her belly, poured imaginary water from a jug.
On the way back she sat with Nati in the back seat of the car, while Greg drove slowly so as not to accidentally hit a pothole on the shoulder with a wheel.
— Beautiful, isn't it, cariño?
— Nati asked Greg.
— Yes, Mom, very beautiful, — said Greg, glancing at how his mother held the woman by both breasts with one hand.
Already as they approached the yard, they saw that another car stood by the gate.
Greg sensed in his gut that something was wrong here.
Nati got out first, asking Greg to take the plaster woman.
He took the statue under the arms, trying not to touch the plaster breasts.
Maria came out to meet them first and said: 'Aiven has stopped by.'
Aiven
The grandmother always called Aiven 'Ivanhoe.'
He came after Ivan, Marko's father.
A photographer with feeble photographs and powerful concepts for shooting the oppressed, a relationship with whom had been wrecked by a dinner at his parents'.

This time Ivanhoe had come not without reason, but supposedly to visit his ex-wife, because they had divorced, despite the conflict with the parents, so peacefully and amicably that the question arose why they were even doing it at all.

Lilichka so sincerely wished him happiness and professional growth (which, in her opinion, he especially needed) that she wanted to introduce him to all her girlfriends, but the only thing that stopped her was that she sometimes told them what a jerk he could be.

And now, when Aiven got out of the car, and beside him — some tall, extravagant lady, Lilichka felt unpleasant for a moment, especially because she was disheveled and unkempt, having spent the last two hours refereeing fights between Roberto and Marko.

Or, to be more precise — pulling Marko off Roberto, and then calming Lusya, who got terribly overexcited by the spectacle and would run to jump in with the boys, until she got Roberto's heel in the face and now had a swollen cheek.
Lilichka looked at herself — flushed, harried — and glanced at this woman beside Aiven, who seemed not to be getting out of a car but floating out of a tanning salon.
The woman stood with some vague smile, holding herself a little apart.
White hair combed and lacquered back, bright red lipstick, a striped off-the-shoulder top and high-waisted black shorts.
Her legs were thin, white and, for some reason, bruised.
Despite the extravagant image, her face looked plain, flat and shallow.

'A Russian woman,' thought Lilichka, peering out from behind the apple tree that still stuck up in the middle of the yard, though the grandmother had long since reclassified it as a tree that posed a danger.
— Marina, — said the woman, holding out her hand when Lilichka came closer.
Lilichka chuckled to herself, and Marina smiled for a moment.
— Privye-e-et, — said Marina, showing even teeth.
— Good afternoon, — retorted Miyechka, who had just come up to the group.
